title: What to Know About Buying a Home With a Well and Septic System on the Oregon Coast
excerpt: Many coastal properties rely on private wells and septic systems instead of city water and sewer. Here's what every buyer should understand before making an offer.
content:
If you're shopping for a home outside city limits on the Southern Oregon Coast, there's a good chance the property uses a private well for drinking water and a septic system for wastewater. This is completely normal in rural areas of Coos County, but it does add some important steps to your buying process that you'll want to understand upfront.

## Why Wells and Septic Are Common Here

Municipal water and sewer services are available in Coos Bay, North Bend, Bandon, and Coquille city limits, but once you step outside those boundaries — which many desirable properties are — you're likely looking at a well and septic setup. This is true for acreage properties, homes along the river corridors, and many homes in the Florence and Brookings areas as well.

## Well Water: What Buyers Should Know

A well provides your home's water supply from an underground aquifer. Before closing on any property with a well, I always recommend:

**Water quality testing** — Test for bacteria (coliform), nitrates, pH levels, and any area-specific concerns. Some coastal wells have naturally elevated iron or manganese, which isn't harmful but can affect taste and stain fixtures.

**Flow rate testing** — This measures how many gallons per minute the well produces. A good residential flow rate is 5 GPM or higher. If the flow rate is low, it could mean expensive improvements down the road.

**Well inspection** — A qualified well technician should inspect the wellhead, casing, pump, and pressure tank. Companies like Barrington Well Drilling in North Bend have been serving the coast for over 65 years and can provide thorough evaluations.

## Septic Systems: What Buyers Should Know

A septic system treats wastewater on your property rather than sending it to a municipal treatment plant. Key things to check:

**Septic inspection** — This is non-negotiable. A certified inspector will locate the tank, check for signs of failure, and evaluate the drain field. A failing septic system can cost $15,000 to $40,000 or more to replace.

**DEQ records** — The Oregon Department of Environmental Quality maintains records on septic permits and inspections. Your inspector can help pull these.

**Tank pumping history** — Septic tanks should be pumped every 3 to 5 years. Ask the seller for maintenance records.

## How I Help My Buyers Navigate This

As your agent, I'll connect you with trusted local professionals who specialize in well and septic inspections. I'll also make sure the right contingencies are in your purchase agreement so you're protected if inspections reveal problems. This is one of those areas where having a local agent who knows the specific challenges of coastal property really matters.

Have questions about a specific property? Reach out anytime — I'm happy to help you evaluate whether a well and septic property is right for you.

title: Spring 2026 Market Update: Southern Oregon Coast
excerpt: A look at what's happening in the Coos Bay, North Bend, Bandon, and Coquille real estate markets as we head into spring 2026.
content:
Spring is traditionally when the Oregon Coast real estate market picks up, and 2026 is shaping up to be an active season. Here's a snapshot of what I'm seeing across the communities I serve.

## Coos Bay

Coos Bay continues to be the most active market on the southern coast. Homes are averaging just 17 days on market, which tells you that well-priced properties in good condition are moving fast. Median home prices have held steady, and buyer demand remains strong — particularly for move-in-ready homes under $400,000.

## North Bend

North Bend is running close behind with an average of 20 days on market. The proximity to the bay, the hospital, and the airport keeps this area popular with both local buyers and people relocating from out of state. Inventory is tight, which means multiple-offer situations are still happening on the most desirable listings.

## Bandon

Bandon's market moves at a different pace, with homes averaging 146 days on market. This isn't a sign of weakness — it reflects the nature of the market. Bandon attracts vacation home buyers and retirees who tend to be more deliberate in their decision-making. Higher price points and seasonal buying patterns are also factors. Well-marketed properties with ocean views or Old Town proximity still generate strong interest.

## Coquille

Coquille offers some of the most affordable housing in the area, with homes averaging 51 days on market. Buyers looking for more space, acreage, or a quieter lifestyle are finding real value here. The 20-minute drive to Coos Bay makes it practical for commuters who want a more rural feel.

## What This Means for Buyers

If you're thinking about buying, especially in Coos Bay or North Bend, being prepared is critical. That means getting pre-approved before you start looking, having your criteria clearly defined, and working with an agent who can move quickly when the right property comes up. I've seen too many buyers lose out on homes because they weren't ready to act.

## What This Means for Sellers

If you've been considering selling, the spring market is historically your strongest window. Low inventory across most of these communities means less competition for your listing. I'd recommend starting the conversation now — even if you're not ready to list until May or June — so we can plan your pricing strategy, get any necessary repairs handled, and have professional photos ready to go.

Want to talk about what your specific property might be worth in today's market? I offer free, no-obligation home value consultations. Reach out anytime.

title: 5 Things That Surprise People About Living on the Oregon Coast
excerpt: Thinking about relocating to the Southern Oregon Coast? Here are a few things that catch newcomers off guard — in the best way.
content:
I talk to people every week who are thinking about moving to the Oregon Coast. Some are retiring from the Willamette Valley, some are remote workers from California or Washington, and some are first-time buyers who grew up visiting Coos Bay or Bandon and always dreamed of living here. No matter where they're coming from, a few things consistently surprise people about what daily life on the coast is actually like.

## 1. The Weather Is Milder Than You Think

Yes, it rains. But the temperature rarely drops below freezing in winter or climbs above 75 in summer. If you're coming from Portland, you already know rain — and the coast actually gets more sunshine than people expect between June and October. The trade-off for a little extra moisture is never needing air conditioning and having some of the freshest air in the country.

## 2. The Cost of Living Is Genuinely Affordable

Compared to Portland, Bend, or anywhere in California, housing on the Southern Oregon Coast is remarkably affordable. You can find well-maintained three-bedroom homes in Coos Bay and North Bend under $350,000, and Coquille offers even more value. Oregon has no sales tax, which stretches your dollar further on everyday purchases.

## 3. The Community Is Tight-Knit

This isn't a tourist-only stretch of coast. Coos Bay, North Bend, Bandon, Coquille, Florence, and Brookings are real working communities with year-round residents, local businesses, and genuine neighborhood connections. People know each other. The barista at your coffee shop will learn your order. Your neighbors will check on you during a storm. That sense of community is something a lot of relocators tell me they've been missing.

## 4. You'll Need to Plan Around Fewer Services

This is the honest part. The Southern Oregon Coast doesn't have a Costco, a Target, or a major hospital with every specialty. Bay Area Hospital in Coos Bay is excellent for most needs, but specialized medical care might require a trip to Roseburg, Eugene, or Portland. Online shopping and delivery services have made this much easier than it used to be, but it's something to factor into your planning.

## 5. The Natural Beauty Never Gets Old

I've lived here for years and I still pull over to watch the sunset. The sea stacks at Bandon, the dunes near Florence, the quiet trails along the Coquille River — these aren't tourist attractions you visit once. They become part of your daily life. Morning walks on the beach, evening kayak runs, crabbing off the docks — it's all right here.

## Thinking About Making the Move?

I help people relocate to the Oregon Coast every month. Whether you're still in the dreaming phase or you're ready to start looking at homes, I'm happy to answer questions about specific communities, neighborhoods, and what life is really like here. No pressure, no sales pitch — just honest local perspective.

title: Preparing Your Home to Sell on the Oregon Coast: A Practical Checklist
excerpt: Getting your coastal property ready for market doesn't have to be overwhelming. Here's my step-by-step checklist for maximizing your sale price.
content:
Selling a home on the Oregon Coast comes with a few unique considerations that you won't find in a generic real estate guide. Salt air, moisture, and coastal weather all leave their marks on a property — and buyers (especially those relocating from drier climates) will notice. Here's my practical checklist for getting your home market-ready.

## Start With Curb Appeal

**Exterior cleaning** — Pressure wash the siding, driveway, walkways, and deck. Coastal homes accumulate moss, algae, and salt film faster than inland properties. A clean exterior immediately signals that the home is well-maintained.

**Roof inspection** — Have a roofer check for moss buildup, missing shingles, and gutter condition. On the coast, roofs take a beating. Buyers will order inspections, and a roof in good shape removes a major negotiation point.

**Landscaping** — Trim overgrown bushes, clean up fallen branches, and add some fresh bark dust or mulch. You don't need a magazine-worthy yard, but tidy and intentional goes a long way.

## Address Moisture Issues

This is the big one for coastal properties. Buyers and inspectors will look closely at:

**Crawl space** — Make sure it's dry, properly vented, and has a vapor barrier in good condition. Moisture in the crawl space is the most common issue I see in coastal home inspections.

**Bathroom ventilation** — Ensure exhaust fans work properly. Replace any that don't. Mold and mildew in bathrooms are red flags for buyers.

**Windows and seals** — Check for condensation between panes and make sure weather stripping is intact. Coastal wind drives rain into every gap.

## Interior Prep

**Declutter ruthlessly** — Less is more. Buyers need to see the space, not your belongings. Pack up personal photos, excess furniture, and anything that makes rooms feel crowded.

**Deep clean** — This means baseboards, light fixtures, inside cabinets, and appliances. Consider hiring a professional cleaning service before listing.

**Touch-up paint** — Fresh paint in neutral tones is the single highest-ROI improvement you can make. It makes everything feel cleaner and more modern.

**Fix the small stuff** — Leaky faucets, sticky doors, burned-out bulbs, cracked outlet covers. These things seem minor, but they add up in a buyer's mind.

## Professional Presentation

Once your home is prepped, I bring in professional photography and, for properties with views or acreage, drone footage. Your listing goes live with a compelling description, strategic pricing based on current market data, and targeted marketing across MLS, Zillow, Realtor.com, social media, and my network of active buyer agents.

## Let's Talk About Your Timeline

If you're thinking about selling in the next few months, the best time to start this conversation is now — not the week you want to list. I'll walk through your home with you, identify the improvements that will actually move the needle on your sale price, and help you prioritize so you're not wasting money on things that don't matter.

Reach out for a free, no-pressure listing consultation. I'll give you an honest assessment of where your home stands and what we can do to get you the best possible result.

title: Understanding Flood Zones on the Southern Oregon Coast
excerpt: Flood zone designations can significantly affect insurance costs and property value. Here's what buyers need to know about flood zones in Coos County.
content:
One of the first things I discuss with buyers looking at property near the coast, rivers, or low-lying areas is flood zone status. It's not the most exciting topic, but understanding flood zones before you make an offer can save you thousands of dollars and prevent major surprises.

## What Are Flood Zones?

FEMA (the Federal Emergency Management Agency) maps flood risk across the country and assigns designations to every property. The main ones you'll encounter on the Southern Oregon Coast are:

**Zone X (Minimal Risk)** — Low to moderate flood risk. Most lenders will not require flood insurance, though you can still purchase it voluntarily.

**Zone A and AE (High Risk)** — These properties are in the 100-year floodplain, meaning there's a 1% chance of flooding in any given year. If you're getting a federally backed mortgage, flood insurance is required — and it's not optional.

**Zone VE (Coastal High Hazard)** — These properties face additional risk from storm surge and wave action. Insurance rates are highest in these zones, and building requirements are more stringent.

## Where Flood Zones Show Up in Our Area

In Coos County, flood zones follow the expected patterns:

- **Along the Coos Bay estuary and waterfront** — Many properties near the bay are in Zone AE
- **Coquille River corridor** — Properties along the river through Coquille and toward Bandon can be in flood zones
- **Low-lying areas near creeks and tributaries** — Even properties that don't seem near water can end up in a flood zone
- **Bandon's coastal areas** — Some oceanfront and near-shore properties carry VE designations

## How Flood Zones Affect Your Purchase

**Insurance costs** — Flood insurance through FEMA's National Flood Insurance Program (NFIP) can range from a few hundred dollars a year to several thousand, depending on the zone and the property's elevation relative to the base flood elevation. This is a real ongoing cost that you need to factor into your monthly budget.

**Financing** — If the property is in a high-risk zone and you're using a conventional, FHA, or VA loan, the lender will require flood insurance as a condition of the mortgage.

**Property value** — Flood zone status doesn't necessarily lower property value, but it does narrow the buyer pool. Some buyers won't consider flood zone properties, which can affect resale.

**Building restrictions** — If you're planning additions or renovations to a flood zone property, you may need to meet additional elevation or construction requirements.

## How I Help Buyers With This

I check flood zone status early in the process — before we even write an offer. I'll pull the FEMA flood map for any property you're interested in and help you understand what the designation means for insurance costs and long-term ownership. If flood insurance is required, I can help you get preliminary quotes so there are no surprises at closing.

Not sure whether a property you've been eyeing is in a flood zone? Send me the address and I'll look it up for you — no strings attached.
